coredata.dc:title: Insight into the effect of synthesis parameters on the properties of graphene quantum dots for theranostic application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Kansara, Vrushti
Patel, Mitali
coredata.dc:description: Ultra-small size and tunable optical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) make them potentially suitable for theranostic applications. This investigation focuses on the effect of process parameters for the synthesis of GQDs of desirable optical properties for biomedical applications. The effect of the raw materials (graphene oxide sheets, glucose, and starch), reaction temperature (170 to 200 °C), and reaction time (3 to 6 h) was investigated on GQDs prepared through hydrothermal treatment. The dispersion of GQDs was characterized using dynamic light scattering (DLS), UV–visible, fluorescence, Raman, and X-ray photon spectroscopic techniques. The effect of the solution temperature and pH on the fluorescence properties of GQDs was also studied. The small hydrodynamic diameter (8.57 ± 1.47 nm) was observed with GQDs prepared from glucose using hydrothermal treatment time of 4 h at 190 °C (GQDs-D). The GQDs-D showed blue fluorescence (λem= 460 nm) with a quantum yield (QY) 67.09 ± 1.25%. The fluorescence characteristic of the GQDs was unchanged up to 6 months during storage at room temperature. GQDs-D revealed their antioxidant nature with a DPPH scavenging activity of 15.08 ± 2.07%. Despite having relatively low DPPH scavenging activity, excellent hemocompatibility (0.23 ± 0.04%) and colloidal stability against bovine serum albumin (BSA) showed GQDs-D as a safe nanocarrier for theranostic application. This was further substantiated with circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy, which exhibited a preserved α-helical structure of BSA by GQDs-D. High-resolution transmission electron microscopic image of GQDs-D showed an average particle size of 5.70 nm and homogeneous particle distribution between 2–8 nm. Conclusively, all the results revealed the potential of GQDs-D for theranostic application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Quantum yield
Fluorescence
Band-gap energy
Oxidative functional groups
Hydrodynamic diameter

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colloidal stability against bovine serum albumin (BSA)
Effect of raw materials, treatment time, and treatment temperature

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful for the financial support provided under the SHODH (Scheme of Developing High Quality Research) scheme to Vrushti Kansara by the Education Department, Government of Gujarat. We would also like to offer our sincere gratitude to IIT Bombay and IIT Sonipat for providing central laboratory facilities for analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the formation of GQDs by the bottom-up technique using glucose as a precursor.
The overlay plot of hydrodynamic diameter of GQDs-A, GQDs-B, GQDs-C, and GQDs-D.

The UV spectrum of GQDs-A depicts characteristic electronic transitions (a), Overlay UV spectra displaying change in optical absorption, and electronic transitions with altered reaction conditions (b).
The change in emission of GQDs with change in diameter under the influence of UV light of 366 nm (a), Emission spectra of GQDs-A, GQDs-B, GQDs-C, and GQDs-D acquired upon excitation at 365 nm (b).
The Tauc plot showing change in band-gap energy of GQDs-A , GQDs-B , GQDs-C and GQDs-D .

The Raman spectra of GQDs-A, GQDs-B, GQDs-C, and GQDs-D. D and G band regions are highlighted in pink and grey shades, respectively (a). The change in ID/IG ratio corresponds to the change in properties of GQDs (b).
The XPS spectra of GQDs-A, GQDs-B, GQDs-C and GQDs-D with C1s (a,c) and O1s (b,d) peaks.
The change in fluorescence intensity of GQDs-A, GQDs-B, GQDs-C and GQDs-D as a function of solution temperature.
The effect of solution pH on fluorescence intensity (a) and zeta potential (b) of GQDs-A, GQDs-B, GQDs-C and GQDs-D.
Photographs of DPPH scavenging activity (a) and hemolytic activity of GQDs-A to GQDs-D (b), Graph showing quantitative value of %DPPH scavenging and hemolytic activity of GQDs-A to GQDs-D (c).
The change in fluorescence intensity (a) and zeta potential (b) of GQDs-A to GQDs-D with increased GQD:BSA ratio.
The CD spectra of BSA acquired upon interaction with GQDs-A, GQDs-B, GQDs-C, and GQDs-D.
HR-TEM image of GQDs-D with log normal distribution displayed in the inset.
The optical properties of GQDs-A, GQDs-B, GQDs-C and GQDs-D.

head.ce:title: Insight into the effect of synthesis parameters on the properties of graphene quantum dots for theranostic application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Maliba Pharmacy College, Uka Tarsadia University, Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Maliba Pharmacy College, Uka Tarsadia University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Maliba Pharmacy College, Uka Tarsadia University, Gujarat, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Maliba Pharmacy College, Uka Tarsadia University, Bardoli 394350, Gujarat, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Maliba Pharmacy College, Uka Tarsadia University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultra-small size and tunable optical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) make them potentially suitable for theranostic applications. This investigation focuses on the effect of process parameters for the synthesis of GQDs of desirable optical properties for biomedical applications. The effect of the raw materials (graphene oxide sheets, glucose, and starch), reaction temperature (170 to 200 °C), and reaction time (3 to 6 h) was investigated on GQDs prepared through hydrothermal treatment. The dispersion of GQDs was characterized using dynamic light scattering (DLS), UV–visible, fluorescence, Raman, and X-ray photon spectroscopic techniques. The effect of the solution temperature and pH on the fluorescence properties of GQDs was also studied. The small hydrodynamic diameter (8.57 ± 1.47 nm) was observed with GQDs prepared from glucose using hydrothermal treatment time of 4 h at 190 °C (GQDs-D). The GQDs-D showed blue fluorescence (λem= 460 nm) with a quantum yield (QY) 67.09 ± 1.25%. The fluorescence characteristic of the GQDs was unchanged up to 6 months during storage at room temperature. GQDs-D revealed their antioxidant nature with a DPPH scavenging activity of 15.08 ± 2.07%. Despite having relatively low DPPH scavenging activity, excellent hemocompatibility (0.23 ± 0.04%) and colloidal stability against bovine serum albumin (BSA) showed GQDs-D as a safe nanocarrier for theranostic application. This was further substantiated with circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy, which exhibited a preserved α-helical structure of BSA by GQDs-D. High-resolution transmission electron microscopic image of GQDs-D showed an average particle size of 5.70 nm and homogeneous particle distribution between 2–8 nm. Conclusively, all the results revealed the potential of GQDs-D for theranostic application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Among different graphene-based materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received considerable attention in biomedical research. GQDs remain attractive as theranostic agent because of their atomic thickness, large surface area, tunable optoelectronic characteristics, chemical inertness, and biocompatibility [1]. As a result of their ultra-small size (<10 nm), the aqueous dispersion of GQDs exhibits high colloidal stability and can be modified to increase drug delivery efficiency [2]. Their resistance against photobleaching has been used for label-free fluorescence-based bioimaging and biosensing [3]. The comparison with carbon nanodots (CNDs) has shown that high crystallinity of GQDs offers superior quantum yield (QY), photostability, and resistance against photobleaching [4].

The electronic and optical properties of GQDs are largely affected by their size and surface chemistry, which affects the band-gap. The small size GQDs possess a wide band-gap and display higher optical absorption and photoluminescence (PL) [5]. Conversely, the band-gap decreases with increase in the size, triggering a shift of PL to a longer wavelength region. It is worth mentioning that the red shift of carefully grown GQDs can be utilized for near-infrared imaging of deep tissue region [6]. A PL shift can also be achieved with the introduction of oxidative functional groups (–OH, -COOH, -CO), which disturb well-defined sp2 hybridized domains [7] and influence the QY of GQDs. This holds significance in determining the competency of GQDs for theranostic application since low QY hampers photostability and leads to inefficiency in bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery monitoring, and diagnosis [8]. Moreover, the size affects the biofate of GQDs. In a comparative study, intravenously injected GQDs were cleared quickly without showing traces of toxicity in mice, whereas graphene oxide (GO) sheets accumulated in the spleen and liver of animals to a lethal level [9].

The size of GQDs can be modulated through synthesis parameters, and it is possible to produce particles with desired properties [10]. For instance, in a typical hydrothermal procedure, this can simply be achieved by optimizing the reaction temperature and time [11]. The size is also affected by the precursor type. For example, GQDs prepared from carbon nanotubes (CNTs) in combination with a high reaction temperature displayed a smaller diameter than those produced from graphene oxide and carbon black. When treated at increased hydrothermal temperature (from 90 to 150 °C), emissive traps associated with surface defects were reduced. As a result, superior PL characteristic was observed [12].

Besides their optical properties, the electron density and oxidative functional groups of GQDs modified through synthesis parameters are also reported to influence their anti-oxidant activity. The antioxidant activity is influenced by several mechanisms, such as electron transfer from the sp2 hybridized graphene backbone and the ability of surface oxidative functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl to donate hydrogen to free radicals [13]. It prohibits oxidative damage to normal cells by stabilizing free radicals like reactive oxygen species (ROS), which play a crucial role in preventing the occurrence of diseases like cancer [14]. Additionally, several reports have described the use of carbon-based nanomaterials as a photosensitizer or pro-oxidant for photodynamic therapy, as they have the ability to kill cancer cell by the production of ROS under ultraviolet light [15]. Moreover, the efficiency of GQDs in drug delivery can also be enhanced through antioxidant activity, as it imparts stability to the drug molecules [16]. As previously mentioned, the antioxidant property is largely dependent on surface oxidative functional groups. Therefore, it is important to modulate GQDs by altering synthesis conditions according to the desired antioxidant activity in order to render them as superior theranostic agent.

Hence, this research was conducted to study the effect of synthesis parameters (amount of raw material, treatment temperature, and time) on the properties of GQDs. Characterizations were performed using dynamic light scattering, UV–visible spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and X-ray photon spectroscopy. Moreover, the free radical scavenging activity, in vitro hemolysis assay, colloidal stability against bovine serum albumin and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy were also performed.

Graphite, glucose, and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were procured from Loba Chemie, India. Starch was procured from S.D. Fine Chemicals Ltd., India. Triton-X 100 and 2,2-diphenylpicrylhydrazyl (DPPH) were purchased from Dual Life Sciences, India. Deionized water was procured from Labdhi Enterprises, India. Other chemicals and materials were of analytical grade.

GQDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment (HTT) using GO, glucose, and starch as precursors. Various amount of GO sheets (1, 3, and 5 g) was subjected to acid exfoliation as described in previous research [17]. The glucose solution was hydrothermally treated in a teflon-lined autoclave [18]. The starch solution was pre-treated at 60 °C for 15 min to allow its conversion into glucose [19]. The temperature and treatment time were varied from 170 to 200 °C and 3 to 6 h, respectively. After HTT treatment, the dispersion was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min, and GQDs were collected.

The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of GQDs were analyzed using Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments, U.K.). The samples were exposed to a He-Ne laser at a scattering angle of 90 °. The equilibrium time of each measurement was set to 30 s.
The optical absorption of the GQD dispersion was determined using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), by taking distilled water as a blank.

The emission of GQDs was monitored using a spectrofluorimeter (Shimadzu RF-5301 PC, Japan). A quinine sulfate solution (0.1 M in H2SO4) was used as a standard. The samples were excited at 365 nm, and the quantum yield (QY) was determined as follows:
(1) %QYsample = %QYstandard x (Isample/Istandard) x (Astandard/Asample) x (ηsample 2/ηstandard 2)
Where, I represents the integrated area of fluorescence peaks. A and η refer to absorbance and refractive index, respectively. The value of η was considered to be 1.33 for samples and standard [20].

The extent of defects and graphitization resulting from change in synthesis conditions were determined using a Raman spectrometer (STR 300, Airix Corporation, Japan). Samples were exposed to a He–Ne laser (532 nm) at a power of 35 mW. Data were acquired in the range of 1000 to 3500 cm−1.

The elements and bonded groups present in GQDs were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (AXIS Supra, Shimadzu, Japan). Samples were dried at room temperature for 2 h before the measurements. Each data is presented as mean ± standard deviation (n=3).

The DPPH scavenging activity of GQDs was assessed using DPPH. The DPPH was dissolved in ethanol (0.005%), and GQDs (0.01%) were added. The mixture was kept in the dark for 1 h, and the absorbance was measured at 515 nm using UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The DPPH scavenging activity was calculated using the following equation [21]:
(2) %DPPH scavenging activity = (A0-Ai /A0) X 100
where, Ai represents the absorbance of DPPH· solution containing GQDs, whereas A0 represents the absorbance of DPPH· solution containing water.

Briefly, blood (2 ml) was collected into a heparin tube and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 20 min at 4°C using a Centrifuge (REMI, C24 Plus). The supernatant was discarded, and the RBC pellet was washed three times with saline (0.9% NaCl solution). The RBCs were then dispersed in saline, GQDs were added to the dispersion and incubated for 1 h at 37 °C. The supernatant was collected through centrifugation for 10 min at 3000 rpm (4°C), and its absorbance was recorded at 540 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The percentage hemolysis against a positive control (20% Triton X-100) was calculated using the following equation [22]:
(3) % Hemolysis = (AS-ANC)/ (APC-ANC) X 100
AS, ANC and APC refer to the absorbance of GQDs, negative control (saline) and positive control, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colloidal stability against bovine serum albumin (BSA)
ce:section.ce:para: The colloidal stability of GQDs was evaluated against BSA with respect to change in fluorescence intensity and zeta potential. Solution of GQDs and BSA in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) pH 7.4 was prepared at different GQD:BSA ratio and incubated at 37 °C. Samples were analysed using spectrofluorimeter (Shimadzu RF-5301 PC, Japan), and Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments, U.K.). The change in the structure of BSA upon surface adsorption of GQDs was examined through circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy. The samples were scanned at 37 °C from 190 to 280 nm at a speed of 500 nm/min using a CD spectrophotometer (JASCO, J-1500, U.K.).

The dispersion of GQDs was drop-casted on a 400 mesh copper grid and dried before the analysis. The data were acquired at an accelerating potential of 200 kV using HR-TEM Microscope (Tecnai G2, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of raw materials, treatment time, and treatment temperature
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of precursor type (GO, glucose, and starch) on the hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) of GQDs was studied (Table S1). The size of the GO-derived GQDs was 966 nm. These GQDs were synthesized after treating GO at 180 °C for 6 h. This demonstrats that GO could not be disintegrated even with harsh treatment. As the amount of GO was increased (from 1 g to 5 g), the size was also increased, resulting in the formation of larger aggregates. Therefore, glucose and starch were selected as raw materials for further experiment. The samples prepared from glucose and starch showed average size of 122.1 and 83.57 nm, respectively, when treated under identical conditions (Fig. S1). The values are considerably lower than those of the GO-derived GQDs, implying that glucose and starch are superior precursors.

The size of these GQDs appears to be influenced by the amount of precursor. The GQDs synthesized with 3 g of glucose were smaller in comparison to the GQDs obtained using 1 g. An incomplete reaction in this case was evident through no colour change in the dispersion and can be attributed to the fact that the precursor is not sufficient to allow the formation of GQDs. In contrast, large aggregates were obtained with 5 g of glucose. The difference in fluorescence was observed through exposure of the dispersion to UV radiation (366 nm). A relatively brighter blue fluorescence was observed with the 3 g of sample, which was decreased when 5 g of precursor was used. Starch-derived GQDs exhibited similar behaviour. It is clearly witnessed that an increase in temperature and treatment time resulted in the reduction in hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) (Table S1) due to the removal of oxidative functional groups such as hydroxyl and epoxy groups at higher temperature. However, an increase in size beyond 190 °C indicates the aggregation of particles. A similar effect was observed with the reaction time beyond 4 h. It is suggested that an extended reaction time facilitates more interactions between the precursor molecules [23]. It was observed that starch-derived GQDs were less stable and showed aggregation after 3 months. In contrast, glucose-derived GQDs were stable with respect to their size and fluorescence at room temperature up to 6 months. The lower stability of the former may possibly be related to its temperature-sensitive change in properties, considering the fact that it requires gelatinization before proceeding with the reaction [24].

Hence, glucose-derived GQDs prepared at hydrothermal temperature of 180 ° and 190 °C with reaction time of 4 and 5 h were further studied to assess change in the properties of GQDs. The mechanism of glucose-derived GQDs using bottom-up hydrothermal synthesis is shown in Fig. 1. GQDs of four different types: GQDs-A (180 °C, 5 h), GQDs-B (180 °C, 4 h), GQDs-C (190 °C, 5 h), and GQDs-D (190 °C, 4 h) were studied for the change in size, chemical composition, electronic transitions, crystallinity, and eventually emission behaviour.

DLS analysis of GQDs-A, GQDs-B, GQDs-C, and GQDs-D shows average hydrodynamic diameter of 42.69 ± 3.76 nm, 18.37 ± 3.35 nm, 15.38 ± 2.05 nm and 8.57 ± 1.47 nm, respectively ( Fig. 2). From the results, it was observed that GQDs obtained at 190 °C showed lower diameter (15.38 ± 2.05 nm and 8.57 ± 1.47 nm) than those obtained at 180 °C (42.69 ± 3.76 nm, 18.37 ± 3.35 nm). This suggests that a significant amount of oxidative functional groups may have been removed from the surface of GQDs at 190 °C. Moreover, with an increased treatment time from 4 to 5 h, the average diameter of GQDs was increased. This is likely due to the greater number of molecules involved in the formation of aggregates as the reaction time proceeds.

Since the DLS plot is determined based on the diffusion of particles, the diffusion of GQDs as a function of size is also represented in fig. S2. The graph showsa linear correlation between the average diameter of GQDs and relaxation time, as larg particles diffuse slowly and thus require more time to relax [25]. In contrast, small-sized GQDs have a shorter relaxation time owing to faster diffusion. The subsequent decline in relaxation time was observed with reduced size of GQDs: 42.69 ± 3.76 nm> 18.37 ± 3.35 nm>15.38 ± 2.05 nm> 8.57 ± 1.47 nm. The results verified a link between the diffusion of particles and hydrodynamic diameter and suggested that an ultra-small size can be obtained via HTT for 4 h at 190 °C. However, detail analysis of the change occurs in each GQD (e.g. change in electronic transitions, structure, and chemistry) is needed to verify the proposed reaction condition, and hence different analytical techniques were performed.

The electronic transitions, or band-gap are highly affected by the size of GQDs. The type of transitions involved with all four GQDs with a change in size was studied using UV–visible spectroscopy. The UV spectrum of GQDs-A ( Fig. 3a) shows characteristic absorption peaks representing π to π * and n to π * transitions at 232 nm and 292 nm, respectively. The π to π * transition is attributed to CC or sp2 hybridized C atoms present in graphene, while the n to π * transition is assigned to CO, oxygenated functional groups attached to graphene backbone [26]. The higher intensity of the peak representing the n to π * transition than the intensity of the peak showing the π to π * transition in GQDs-A suggested the presence of a higher number of oxygenated functional groups (-COOH). As the size of GQDs reduced, the n to π * transition was also decreased which was accompanied by the blue-shift of the absorption peak related to π to π * transitions (Fig. 3b). This indicates enhanced sp2 hybridization with reduction in size, affected by change in treatment time and temperature.

A detail regarding change in transitions is represented via the deconvoluted spectra of GQDs-A to GQDs-D (Fig. S3). Two deconvoluted peaks are assigned to show π to π * and n to π * transitions. From the UV spectrum of GQDs-A (Fig. S3a), it was observed that the n to π * transition was predominant, which was represented by a higher peak intensity. With a decrease in reaction time from 5 to 4 h, (Fig. S3b) the intensity of a peak representing n to π * transitions was comparatively decreased, possibly due to the removal of a larger amount of oxygenated functional groups. Moreover, the lower intensity of the peak showing the π to π * transition in both cases supported the assumption of the presence of a higher number of defects. This shows that the defects of the GQDs can be modulated by reducing treatment time in order to improve fluorescence property. In the case of GQDs obtained at 190 °C (GQDs-C and GQDs-D), both peak was shifted to the lower wavelength compared to GQDs-A and GQDs-B (Fig. S3c,d). This blue shift indicates increased sp2 hybridization and the presence of a high density of delocalized π electrons [27]. It can clearly be seen that both the transitions are noticeably increased with a reduction in treatment time. Moreover, the intensity of peaks representing respective transitions in the case of GQDs-D displayed the co-existence of sp2 hybridized C atoms and oxygenated functional groups in nearly equal proportion, which could enhance fluorescence property through the combination effect of inherent and defect-related fluorescence. These observations also indicate the possibility of increased QY values, which were further confirmed via fluorescence spectroscopy.

A significant difference in the fluorescence of GQDs of different size (GQDs-A to GQDs-D) is clearly witnessed in Fig. 4a. GQDs-A to GQDs-D were irradiated at 366 nm using UV light, where GQDs-D were more illuminated with blue light than the relatively large-sized GQDs-A, GQDs-B, and GQDs-C. This may be attributed to the quantum confinement effect and the surface chemistry of GQDs-D. GQDs-A showed slight illumination at the surface of the solution, which was improved with a reduction in size (GQDs-B) and showed light blue fluorescence throughout the solution. This revealed the importance of controlling treatment time. GQDs-C and GQDs-D, produced at 190 °C, exhibited more intense illumination than GQDs-A and GQDs-B, which were prepared at 180 °C. This indicates that the temperature of the reaction considerably influences the emission of GQDs. The fluorescence of GQDs-D was improved and displayed higher illumination than the other three: GQDs-A, GQDs-B, and GQDs-C. This may be due to the defects associated with the amount of oxygenated functional groups present in the respective GQDs since it is reported that an abundance of oxygenated functional groups present on the surface of GQDs resulted in increased sp3 hybridization, which in turn diminish the fluorescence intensity [28]. Hence, the brightness of the emitted light was distinct with each GQD synthesized from different reaction conditions. The emission spectra of GQDs-A to GQDs-D, acquired at λex of 365 nm, supported this observation. The intensity of λem at 460 nm (blue region) was increased from GQDs-A to GQDs-D (Fig. 4b). Enhanced intensity of the GQDs-D in the blue region can be related to the quantum confinement effect and abundance of delocalized π electrons or sp2 hybridized graphene structure. The increase in π electrons reduces the non-radiative recombination of electron-hole pair associated with structural defects, which in turn increases the fluorescence intensity [28]. The relatively lower intensity of GQDs-A, GQDs-B, and GQDs-C can be assigned to an increased number of oxygenated functional groups (mainly –COOH) that contribute to defects and eventually cause non-radiative recombination [29].

It is important to calculate the change in band-gap energy, as the quantum confinement effect is one of the possible mechanisms responsible for the altered emission. The band-gap energy was determined through Tauc plots sourced from UV spectra ( Fig. 5). The band-gap energy was found to be increased from GQDs-A to GQDs-D ( Table 1). The band gap energy of GQDs shifts to higher energy with reducing quantum dots size, from 4.086 to 5.670 eV. This indicates that quantum confinement effect also contributes to the fluorescence intensity.

The QY value of GQDs was found to be increased from 10.16 ± 2.05 to 67.09 ± 1.25% (Table 1). This may be ascribed to the increased sp2 hybridization in GQDs-D, which was resulted from the removal of an abundance of oxygenated functional groups. It is also reported that higher QY values are linked to high crystallinity which represents increased sp2 hybridization [30] in contrast to the low QY values that originate from the defects related to a high amount of oxidative functional groups (–COOH). Hence, the higher QY value in GQDs-D is indicative of increased crystallinity in comparison to GQDs-A, GQDs-B, and GQDs-C which is further demonstrated by Raman spectroscopy.

The extent of the defect in each GQD was identified via the ID/IG ratio, where the intensity of the D and G bands was used. The D and G bands are characteristic peaks for graphene-based nanomaterials. Since GQDs are composed of a graphene backbone, structural properties can be identified using the vibrational modes of graphene. The D band is assigned for defects, generally owing to the presence of an abundance of oxygenated functional groups, whereas the G band represents graphitization resulting from the amount of π electron clouds present in the graphene backbone [31]. GQDs with varied synthesis conditions showed modifications in graphitization or crystallinity ( Fig. 6a).

All the GQDs showed D and G bands around 1358 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 respectively, with altered intensity (Fig. 6a). The D band intensity was obtained in the order of GQDs-A>GQDs-B>GQDs-C>GQDs-D. Contrary to this, the G band intensity was obtained in the order of GQDs-A<GQDs-B<GQDs-C<GQDs-D. This indicates relatively higher crystallinity of GQDs-D as the sharp amplification of the G band intensity reflects increased sp2 hybridization. The observations are also supported by the ID/IG ratio (Fig. 6b). GQDs-A and GQDs-D showed values of 7.12 and 0.213, respectively (Table 1). This shows increased crystallinity and sp2 hybridization of GQDs-D which contributes to increased emission in GQDs-D.

Since it is speculated that the amount of oxygenated functional groups and sp2 clusters contribute to the emission behaviour, the XPS study was performed to verify the observations of previous analysis. The wide spectrum overlay confirmed the presence of C and O in all four types of GQDs, with varied intensity of C1s and O1s peaks (Fig. S4). The detail analysis of C1s and O1s peaks is shown in Fig. 7. The C1s and O1s peaks of GQDs-A and GQDs-B showed lower intensity in comparison to GQDs-C and GQDs-D. C1s showed peaks of CC representing sp2 domains (283.8 eV), C-O groups (286 eV) and minor extended peak of CO (291.5 eV) [32]. The intensity of CC peak of GQDs-B is higher than GQDs-A and CO peak is negligible in GQDs-B (Fig. 7a). This confirms the moderate removal of defects and increased crystallinity in GQDs-B. The O1s region showed a peak related to the presence of the –COOH functional group, as indicated by the CO peak at 531.8 eV. The high intensity of this peak demonstrates that a large amount of –COOH is attached to the surface of GQDs-A (Fig. 7b). The intensity of CC peak is slightly higher in GQDs-D in reference to GQDs-C and the peak of other oxygenated functional groups are absent (Fig. 7c). However, the hump in C1s peak of GQDs-C demonstrates the presence of defects which is absent in GQDs-D. Also, the considerably higher intensity of CO peak in GQDs-C than GQDs-D indicates increased sp2 clusters in GQDs-D (Fig. 7d). These observations are in line with the results of Raman analysis and verified that the high QY of GQDs-D is associated with increased graphitization.

The result showed that glucose-derived GQDs had maximum emission at 30 °C. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs was low at temperature lower and higher than 30 °C ( Fig. 8). This is ascribed to reduced radiative recombination resulting from formation of larger aggregates. At temperatures higher than 30 °C, the energy band-gap of GQDs is expanded which increases random motions and collisions of electrons during electronic transitions. Hence, it produced large aggregates and eventually diminished the fluorescence of GQDs [33]. Moreover, the crystalline structure of GQDs started to distort with subsequent increase in temperature. It eventually reduced electron-hole pair radiative recombination which led to completely diminish fluorescence at 60 °C [34]. Nonetheless, the diminished fluorescence intensity varied with each GQD (Fig. 8) on the account of their distinct structural and chemical properties. Contrast results were obtained in the case of starch-derived GQDs where maximum emission was witnessed at low temperature (4 °C). This is ascribed to the presence of high number of radiative recombination of electron-hole pair which are not disturbed at this temperature. In line with this, the hydrodynamic size of GQDs obtained from glucose and starch was not significantly changed at room temperature and 4 °C, respectively. The emission pattern was maintained with a negligible difference up to 6 months in glucose-derived GQDs, while aggregates started appearing in the solution of starch-derived GQDs along with a significant change in the emission pattern after 3 months.

The fluorescence property and size of GQDs were not significantly affected at pH 7. Relatively low fluorescence intensity was observed at pH 3, 5, 8 and 12 ( Fig. 9a). As the GQDs were synthesized at neutral pH, it contributed to high fluorescence intensity [35]. The size of GQDs was also unaffected at neutral pH, compared to other pH conditions where the size is significantly increased. The fluorescence intensity at alkaline pH was reduced compared to that of acidic pH (Fig. 9a). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity was reduced with increasing pH (3 to 5) due to a diminished extent of protonation. The subsequent reduction in fluorescence intensity at pH 8 and 12 is ascribed to the effect of deprotonation [36]. Protonation offers a higher number of functional groups responsible for radiative recombination compared to the relatively higher amount of -COO- obtained at alkaline pH through deprotonation [37]. It indicates a change in structural integrity which ultimately led to reduction in fluorescence property. The graphs also depicted the comparative difference in GQDs synthesized from varied reaction conditions. However, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-C is near to GQDs-D at pH 3 and 7. This may be due to a minute difference in charge transfer at pH 3 and 7, which can be seen by zeta potential value (Fig. 9b). A similar pattern was observed in the case of GQDs-B and GQDs-C at pH 8 and 12.

The effect of protonation and deprotonation was also reflected via the altered zeta potential of GQDs. A change in pH of a solution affects charge transfer ability, which is considered an ability of GQDs to donate or accept electrons. As per the graph (Fig. 9b), GQDs-A shows a zeta potential of − 3.95 mV at acidic pH 3, which changed to − 9.56 mV at pH 7 and − 15.7 mV at alkaline pH 12. This change is due to altered charge transfer of carboxyl functional groups available on the surface of GQDs. At an acidic pH, protonation takes place, which imparts positive charge to carboxyl functional groups and hence shifts zeta potential of GQDs towards the positive side compared to zeta potential observed at neutral pH. In contrast, carboxyl groups receive more negative charge with increased pH owing to deprotonation and hence showed a subsequent increase in negative charge [38]. In both the cases, fluorescence property is reduced in comparison to the ones observed at neutral pH, as the moieties produced at acidic and alkaline pH increase non-radiative recombinations. The varied zeta potential among four different types of GQDs is due to the distinct amount of surface carboxylic functional groups that actively participate in charge transfer. GQDs-A with a high amount of carboxyl functional groups showed a prominent change in zeta potential values than GQDs-B, GQDs-C and GQDs-D. GQDs-D showed a relatively lower extent of protonation and deprotonation than GQDs-A, GQDs-B and GQDs-C due to the less oxidative functional groups available on the surface. There was a negligible change in fluorescence intensity at neutral pH up to 6 months (Fig. S5).

The excessive production of ROS can hamper normal cellular functions and, if not neutralized, may create a physiological environment suitable for various deadly diseases like cancer. It is crucial to develop and evaluate materials that can effectively balance ROS production in healthy cells and reduce oxidative stress. As previously mentioned, GQDs possess antioxidant activity depending on the extent of the conjugated carbon structure and defects associated with surface oxidative functional groups, which are related to sp2 and sp3 hybridization, respectively. Thus, GQDs of varied oxidative functional groups (GQDs-A to GQDs-D) were evaluated for their ROS scavenging potential using DPPH free radicals. The antioxidant activity is physically witnessed through a change in color from violet to yellow when a free radical is neutralized via H atom binding and single electron transfer [39]. The extent of the antioxidant activity of GQDs-A to GQDs-D was evident through a change in yellow color where GQDs-A showed a dark yellow color which subsequently reduced to light yellow from GQDs-B to GQDs-D ( Fig. 10a). The %DPPH scavenging activity was also reduced from GQDs-A to GQDs-D (87.32 ± 2.05 to 15.08 ± 2.07%) (Fig. 10c). The high antioxidant activity of GQDs-A is ascribed to a high amount of oxidative functional groups that offer a high number of H atoms for free radical neutralisation, along with a few sp2 hybridized aromatic regions [13]. The %DPPH scavenging activity was found in order of GQDs-A>GQDs-B>GQDs-C>GQDs-D with a reduction in the amount of surface oxidative functional groups (-COOH) or defects. This indicates the dominating effect of the surface oxidative functional groups in determination of antioxidant activity rather than combinational effect of oxidative functional groups and sp2 hybridized aromatic regions [40]. GQDs-A, with a relatively high number of defects as proven by the ID/IG ratio, are able to generate a greater number of active sites to scavenge free radicals [21]. This shows direct correlation between DPPH scavenging activity and extent of defects. As hydrogen atom transfer is the dominant mechanism in this case, GQDs-D was found to have relatively low DPPH scavenging activity despite having an abundance of π electron clouds that could contribute to high DPPH scavenging activity via the electron transfer mechanism. However, it is not the only factor that determines the overall antioxidant potential of a compound. There are some other factors that contribute to the low DPPH scavenging activity, such as concentration of a molecule [41]. Phenolic compounds, which are known for their high π electron systems, exhibited low antioxidant activity due to their low concentration compared to other small-molecule antioxidants [42]. The other factor includes a stable bond (e.g., crystalline nature) of GQDs-D, which makes them structurally stable and obstructs their connectivity with free radicals, eventually reducing DPPH scavenging activity [21]. These observations exhibited the potential of GQDs as antioxidants, but the extent differed with the chemistry of GQDs, and confirmed that the antioxidant activity is ultimately associated with synthesis parameters.

The direct administration of GQDs into the bloodstream can negatively affect RBCs by inducing severe damage to the RBC membrane and reducing the hemoglobin level. The amount of hemoglobin released as a result of RBC damage is measured to evaluate the hemocompatibility of any nanomaterials, including GQDs. Hence, GQDs-A to GQDs-D were assessed for their hemolytic activity. The GQDs-A was found to have 1.61 ± 0.05% of hemolytic activity, which was reduced to 0.23 ± 0.04% with GQDs-D (Fig. 10c). The difference in hemolytic activity may be due to the distinct number of surface functional groups. A high amount of oxidative functional groups may cause excessive damage to RBCs by inducing oxidative stress [43] and a similar reason can apply for GQDs-A having relatively high hemolytic activity, considering their potential as pro-oxidants. However, the values comply with the standard stated by the American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM E2524–08 standard) (<5%), and all GQDs are confirmed to be hemocompatible which is also evident through photographs (Fig. 10b).

The superiority of GQDs lies in their inherent fluorescence properties, which need to be retained in a physiological environment. With such concern, the colloidal stability of GQDs was tested against BSA protein. GQDs-A to GQDs-D showed no change in the emission wavelength of GQDs over 24 h when excited at 365 nm. This indicates no considerable change in the fluorescence property of GQDs or the structure of BSA. However, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was observed to be decreased with increased incubation time on account of increased π-π interaction between the graphene backbone and the aromatic region of BSA over a period of 24 h (Fig. S6).

In addition to this, fluorescence intensity was reduced when GQDs were exposed to an increased concentration of BSA (up to a GQD:BSA ratio of 1:4). After this point, the fluorescence intensity was increased with a further increase in the GQD:BSA ratio ( Fig. 11a). This can be attributed to the concentration-dependent adsorption and desorption of BSA. The observations were substantiated by a change in the zeta potential values of GQDs. There was a consequent shift in the zeta potential with an increase in the GQD:BSA ratio (from 1:0 to 1:4) (Fig. 11b). At 1:0 ratio, all four types of GQDs displayed negative zeta potential values: − 12.09 ± 2.02, − 8.01 ± 1.03, − 6.63 ± 0.79, and − 4.59 ± 0.89 mV, assigned to GQDs-A, GQDs-B, GQDs-C, and GQDs-D, respectively. As the concentration of BSA was increased , the zeta potential of GQDs was shifted towards a positive side. For instance, the zeta potential of GQDs-A shifted from − 12.09 ± 2.02 to − 8.03 ± 1.20 to 3.41 ± 1.30 mV when the concentration of BSA was increased. This is attributed to the adsorption of BSA, resulting from the electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged GQDs and the positively charged lysine fraction of BSA, along with hydrophobic interactions [44]. With an increased concentration of BSA, the adsorption of BSA also increased, which ultimately shifted the zeta potential towards a positive side as BSA covers the overall surface of GQDs. This pattern is observed until the GQD:BSA ratio reached to 1:4. GQDs-A displayed a zeta potential value of 9.72 ± 1.02 mV at this ratio. After that, the zeta potential again shifted to a negative side. GQDs-A showed a change in zeta potential value from 9.72 ± 1.02 mV to − 6.62 ± 1.10 mV with a further increase in the concentration of BSA. This change is attributed to the desorption of BSA from the surface of GQDs. The desorption occurs due to increased repulsive forces between adsorbed BSA molecules at high concentration. This alters the orientation of BSA, which is unfavourable for interaction with GQDs, and detachment of BSA takes place from the surface of GQDs. This initiates exposure of the surface of GQDs and the negative charge of GQDs is resumed [45]. The varied adsorption of BSA on the surface of GQDs is attributed to the different amount of surface oxidative functional groups of GQDs involved in the electrostatic interaction, and hence, despite such interaction, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-D was not largely hampered compared to GQDs-A, GQDs-B, and GQDs-C.

The interaction between GQDs and BSA also hints at the possibility of an altered structure of BSA. Even with no shift in λem, it is important to identify the effect of GQDs on the structure of BSA, as the impeded protein structure may lead to several toxicities. To monitor change in the structure of BSA, CD spectra of BSA incubated with GQDs-A to GQDs-D were acquired. The secondary structure of BSA is provided through the CD spectrum of BSA ( Fig. 12). The CD spectra of GQDs-D was identical to the secondary structure of BSA with reduced intensity. The subsequent reduction in intensity from GQDs-D to GQDs-B, indicating interaction between GQDs and a fraction of BSA containing amino acids, with no damage to the α-helical structure of BSA. A complete change in pattern was witnessed upon the adsorption of BSA on the surface of GQDs-A. It is recognised as an extensive change in protein configurations or the unfolding of the α-helical structure [46]. It is thus clearly evident that a relatively high amount of surface oxidative functional groups in GQDs-A possess the ability to damage the protein structure owing to a high electrostatic interaction with BSA. This also substantiated the superiority of GQDs-D for safe administration.
The results from all spectroscopic analyses displayed the superiority of GQDs-D for theranostic application.

The HR-TEM image ( Fig. 13) showed spherical-shape, uniformly distributed GQDs-D with size ranging between 2 and 8 nm and a maximum %count of 5.70 nm, which can clearly be seen in the inset of Fig. 13. The ultra-small size of GQDs-D approached a size equal to the exciton Bohr radius, which activated the quantum confinement effect [47] indicating influence of size on the optical properties of GQDs-D.

The applications of GQDs are determined by the properties of GQDs, specifically through tunable optical properties that are majorly influenced by synthesis conditions. The GQDs were successfully prepared by a bottom-up approach using glucose. The analytical studies depicted a considerable change in optical properties resulting from altered oxidative functional groups (extent of defects) and sp2 hybridization. The high QY value, stable fluorescence, and low hemolytic activity of GQDs-D proved their superiority for long-term fluorescence-based bioimaging, biosensing, and drug delivery. The DPPH scavenging activity could contribute to the enhancement of drug delivery efficiency and anticancer activity through ROS neutralization capacity. Moreover, the structure of BSA did not deteriorate in the presence of GQDs-D, indicating their potential for theranostic application from a safety perspective. Overall, this study reveals a clear correlation between synthesis parameters and various properties of GQDs. Hence, the results showed that GQDs can be utilized for theranostic application.
Patel Mitali: Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Kansara Vrushti: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interests.

We would also like to offer our sincere gratitude to IIT Bombay and IIT Sonipat for providing central laboratory facilities for analysis.
